content: Respiratory viruses may affect both the upper respiratory tract (the vocal cords and above) and the lower respiratory tract (below the vocal cords). Some viruses that affect the nose and throat can progress to cause serious lung infections, such as pneumonia. Symptoms affecting the respiratory tract include: runny or stuffy nose, new loss of taste or smell, sore throat, and cough. Other possible symptoms include: headache, muscle or body aches, fever, chills, fatigue, weakness, decrease in appetite, vomiting or diarrhea. What to do when sick: Stay home and away from others until all symptoms are getting better and you have not had a fever for at least 24 hours without fever-reducing medication.

Respiratory illnesses can make anyone sick. However, some people have risk factors that increase their chances of getting very sick. Age is a key factor: adults 65 years and older and children younger than 2 years old are at higher risk. Underlying conditions such as chronic lung disease, heart disease, diabetes, or chronic kidney disease, a weakened immune system, disability, and pregnancy also increase risk of severe illness.

If you or your child have an emergency warning sign, seek emergency medical care immediately. Emergency warning signs in ADULTS include: difficulty breathing or shortness of breath, persistent pain or pressure in the chest or abdomen, persistent dizziness or confusion or inability to arouse, seizures, not urinating, severe muscle pain, severe weakness or unsteadiness, high fever, symptoms that improve but then return or worsen. Emergency warning signs in CHILDREN include: fast breathing or trouble breathing, bluish lips or face (cyanosis), ribs pulling in with each breath, chest pain, severe muscle aches, dehydration, not alert or interacting when awake, seizures, fever above 104 degrees F, symptoms that improve but then return or worsen.

Timely treatment of a respiratory virus can help make symptoms less severe and prevent complications, especially for those at higher risk. Seek healthcare right away for testing and/or treatment if you feel sick or tested positive for a respiratory virus and have risk factors for severe illness. If you have flu or COVID-19, treatment may be an option to make your symptoms less severe and shorten the time you are sick. Treatment needs to be started within a few days of when your symptoms begin.

Treatments for COVID-19 and flu can lessen symptoms and shorten the time you are sick. They also may reduce the risk of complications, including those that can result in hospitalization. For people with risk factors for severe illness, early treatment can mean having milder illness. Antiviral medications may be an option to help reduce your risk of serious illness. Antivirals are taken for viral infections like flu and COVID-19. Antibiotics are taken for bacterial infections. Antiviral medications and antibiotics are not sold over the counter — you need a prescription from a healthcare provider.

Know the treatment options for flu and COVID-19 before you get sick. Talk with a healthcare provider about planning to access treatment in advance. Take all treatments as prescribed. If you are at higher risk for severe illness, seek healthcare promptly — do not wait to see if symptoms improve on their own. Early treatment is especially important for older adults, people with chronic conditions, and those with weakened immune systems.

Stay home and away from others if you have respiratory virus symptoms that are not better explained by another cause. Symptoms include fever, chills, fatigue, cough, runny nose, and headache among others. You can return to normal activities when, for at least 24 hours, both are true: your symptoms are getting better overall, AND you have not had a fever without using fever-reducing medication. When you return to normal activities, take added precautions for the next 5 days such as improved air quality measures, good hygiene, wearing a mask, physical distancing, or testing before being around other people.

Monitor your symptoms closely while sick. If you have an emergency warning sign such as trouble breathing or chest pain, seek emergency medical care immediately. If your symptoms improve but then return or get worse, stay home again and monitor carefully. People with weakened immune systems can spread the virus for a longer period of time even after feeling better. A positive COVID-19 test tends to mean it is more likely you can spread the virus to others. Taking precautions is especially important to protect people with risk factors for severe illness.

Respiratory virus symptoms may include but are not limited to: chest discomfort, chills, cough, decrease in appetite, diarrhea, fatigue, fever or feeling feverish, headache, muscle or body aches, new loss of taste or smell, runny or stuffy nose, sneezing, sore throat, vomiting, weakness, wheezing. If you tested positive but never had symptoms, you may still be contagious. Take added precautions for the next 5 days.

source: CDC - Clinical Overview of Respiratory Illnesses (HCP)
content: Assess whether the patient has risk factors that place them at higher risk for severe flu and COVID-19 and should receive prescription antiviral medications if they become ill. Patients should receive all recommended vaccines, testing, and any needed treatment in a timely manner. For outpatients with acute respiratory illness at higher risk of severe COVID-19 and/or influenza, clinical guidance recommends early evaluation and treatment. High-risk patients include older adults, those with chronic conditions, and immunocompromised individuals.

source: CDC - Clinical Overview of Respiratory Illnesses (HCP)
content: Testing for COVID-19 and influenza should be considered when these viruses are circulating in the community, especially for patients with risk factors for severe illness. Multiplex assays are available for simultaneous detection of influenza viruses and SARS-CoV-2. Rapid influenza diagnostic tests are available for quick results. Testing helps ensure patients do not delay treatment and allows appropriate isolation to protect family and contacts. Early identification through testing enables timely antiviral treatment, which is most effective when started within the first few days of symptom onset.

source: CDC - Clinical Overview of Respiratory Illnesses (HCP)
content: CDC recommends updated COVID-19 vaccine for most adults 18 years and older. Everyone 6 months and older should receive a seasonal influenza vaccine. For RSV: all infants should be protected through vaccination of pregnant women (32-36 weeks gestation) or immunization of infants less than 8 months with long-acting RSV monoclonal antibody. Adults 75 and older should receive RSV vaccination. Adults 50-74 with certain medical conditions or risk factors should also receive RSV vaccination. Flu, COVID-19, and RSV vaccines may be co-administered at the same visit.

source: CDC - People at Increased Risk for Severe Respiratory Illnesses
content: People at higher risk of severe illness from respiratory viruses include: older adults, young children, people with certain medical conditions (including those with weakened immune systems and people with disabilities), and pregnant women. If you or someone you spend time with is at higher risk, taking protective actions is especially important. If you believe you may have a respiratory virus and have risk factors for severe illness, seek healthcare right away for testing and treatment. Treatment needs to be started within a few days of symptom onset to be most effective.

source: CDC - People at Increased Risk for Severe Respiratory Illnesses
content: As people get older, their immune systems can weaken over time, increasing risk for complications from respiratory illnesses. Older adults are also more likely to have underlying health conditions. Most deaths from respiratory viruses occur in people older than 65, with risk increasing sharply with advancing age. Adults 65 and older should seek healthcare promptly when experiencing respiratory symptoms, as early treatment is critical for this group.

source: CDC - People at Increased Risk for Severe Respiratory Illnesses
content: Young children, particularly infants, have immune systems that are still developing. Their lungs and airways are smaller, making respiratory viruses more dangerous. Children younger than 2 years are at higher risk for severe illness. Special considerations: children younger than 12 years cannot use Paxlovid for COVID-19. Remdesivir is a treatment option for high-risk children from birth to 18 years. Oseltamivir (Tamiflu) is approved for flu treatment in children 14 days and older. Speak with a healthcare provider about treatment options if your child develops respiratory symptoms.

source: CDC - People at Increased Risk for Severe Respiratory Illnesses
content: Certain medical conditions increase risk for severe illness from respiratory viruses: chronic lung disease, heart disease, diabetes, chronic kidney disease, weakened immune system, disability, and pregnancy. These conditions weaken the body's ability to fight infection or affect organs also impacted by respiratory illness. People with weakened immune systems (immunocompromise) have lower defenses against infections and may have difficulty building lasting protection from vaccination or prior infection. Examples include cancer treatment, organ transplant with immunosuppressive therapy, and primary immunodeficiency. These individuals should seek medical care promptly at the first sign of respiratory symptoms.
